label.publicEndpoint: Configure this URL in Claude Desktop, Cursor, or any compatible MCP client.
label.authEndpoint: Configure this URL in your MCP client and authenticate with either:
label.noKeys: No keys — any MCP client can connect without authentication.
label.scanQr: Scan to open on a mobile device
label.shareValidityPermanent: This link stays valid until the share slug changes. It gives read-only setup information — no access to data or credentials.
label.shareValidity: This link is valid for 30 days. It gives read-only setup information — no access to data or credentials.
label.noOAuthClient: OAuth is disabled. The MCP endpoint can still use Access Keys or remain public.
label.serverPaused: Server is paused — AI cannot use it right now
label.aiCanCallTools: Your AI assistant can call tools in this server.
label.allRequestsReturn503: All MCP requests return a 503 error until you resume.
label.noInputsRequired: No inputs required — the URI is fixed.
label.noContent: No content yet — write HTML in the Code tab.
label.autoDetectedInputs: Auto-derived chain inputs (exposed to the MCP client)
label.blankResourceDesc: Static content — write the HTML manually
label.maintenanceMessageToClients: Message shown to clients when maintenance is active
label.retryMaxWaitPrefix: With {{retries}} retries and {{strategy}} backoff starting at {{delay}}ms, the maximum wait before giving up is approximately
label.timeoutAppliesAll: = {{seconds}}s — applies to all tools without an override

placeholder.searchByPathNameDesc: Search by path, name or description…
placeholder.resourceUriHelp: Unique identifier used by the MCP client to read this resource
placeholder.describeParam: Describe this parameter so the AI knows what to pass…
placeholder.dragOrBrowse: Drag spec here or click to browse
placeholder.regexMatch: Regular expression the value must match

hint.baseUrl: Base URL used for all HTTP calls in this server
hint.publicDocSlugFormat: Used in generated share links as
hint.publicDocSlugUnique: It must be unique across servers.
hint.shareSlugPattern: Use lowercase letters, numbers, and hyphens.
hint.baseUrlOverride: Leave blank to use the URL declared in the spec
hint.apiBaseUrlHelp: The root address of the external API this server connects to.
hint.keyLabel: A label to identify which client uses this key
hint.uriUnique: Unique identifier used by the MCP client to read this resource
hint.resourceUriHelp: Unique identifier used by the MCP client to read this resource
hint.errorMessageHelp: Shown to the MCP client if the API call fails. Use {{error}} to include the original error.
hint.rateLimitActive: Limits MCP server calls to {{rpm}} req/min. Exceeding the limit returns HTTP 429.
hint.rateLimitInactive: No request limit. Enable to restrict usage per minute.
hint.responseLimitActive: Response truncation active. The AI receives capped responses according to the configured limits.
hint.responseLimitInactive: No response limits. The AI receives the full raw response from the upstream API.
hint.maintenancePlaceholder: We are performing scheduled maintenance. Back online at 14:00 UTC.
hint.maintenanceHint: When enabled, all requests get a 503 with this message. Useful for planned downtime.
hint.availabilityHint: Restrict AI access to specific days and hours. Requests outside the schedule return a 503 error.
hint.tenantHint: Only tools that declare these parameters in their spec will receive the values. Others are unaffected.
hint.handlebarsHint: Write HTML using Handlebars.js syntax, use {{variable}} to insert data fields and {{#each items}} to iterate over arrays.
hint.handlebarsTransform: Transform the API response into HTML using Handlebars.js
hint.handlebarsEnableToggle: Enable the toggle to transform the API JSON response into an HTML page using Handlebars.js.
hint.alertHint: Checks every 15 minutes. At most one alert every 30 minutes per project.
hint.tenantParamInject: Inject query string values into tool calls that accept them.
hint.chainDesc: Sequential tool compositions that appear as a single MCP tool. Each step's output is available to the next step.
hint.accessKeyHint: Named keys that control who can connect to this server's AI endpoint.
hint.accessKeyTransports: Clients may send a key in the auth header or the auth query parameter; requests without a valid key are rejected.
hint.oneKeyPerClient: Create one key per client (for example, Claude Desktop or Cursor) so access can be revoked independently.
hint.queryAccessKeyWarning: Prefer the header when possible. Query-string keys may appear in browser history and infrastructure logs.
hint.publicAccess: Without any key, the endpoint is publicly accessible to anyone who knows the URL.
hint.oauthHint: Allows ChatGPT (and other OAuth 2.0 clients) to connect to this server's MCP endpoint.
hint.oauthClientPurpose: Protects this MCP endpoint with OAuth 2.0 so compatible AI clients can authenticate before calling tools.
hint.oauthHelpIntro: Use this section to control how ChatGPT, Claude, and other MCP clients authenticate before using this server's tools. It protects incoming MCP access; it does not configure the credentials used by Arthur to call the upstream API.
hint.oauthHelpChooseExternal: External OAuth/OIDC: recommended when your customer has their own website, users, login, consent, or MFA. The login page belongs to their identity provider.
hint.oauthHelpChooseManaged: Arthur-managed OAuth: use for internal environments or testing. Arthur displays the login page and issues the access token.
hint.oauthHelpChooseDisabled: Disabled: use when this MCP endpoint should rely only on Access Keys or remain public.
hint.oauthHelpExternalStep1: In the customer's authorization server, register the MCP client and allow Authorization Code with PKCE. Add the callback URLs required by the AI client being used.
hint.oauthHelpExternalStep2: Enter the exact Issuer URL and the provider's Authorization and Token URLs. These pages and endpoints must belong to the customer.
hint.oauthHelpExternalStep3: For JWT access tokens, enter the JWKS URL. For opaque tokens, enter the Introspection URL and its confidential Client ID and Client Secret. Configure at least one validation method.
hint.oauthHelpExternalStep4: Set the provider's Audience/Resource to this MCP resource and enter the same value here: {{resource}}. Tokens issued for another API will be rejected.
hint.oauthHelpExternalStep5: Enter every scope required to use this MCP server, separated by spaces or commas. A token missing any listed scope will be rejected.
hint.oauthHelpExternalStep6: Save the configuration. In the AI client, configure the server using the Connection URL shown above; compatible clients discover the OAuth provider automatically.
hint.oauthHelpDiscovery: OAuth discovery starts at {{url}}. Open this URL to inspect the resource, authorization server, and supported scopes published to MCP clients.
hint.oauthHelpManagedStep1: Select Arthur-managed OAuth, generate or enter a Client ID and Client Secret, and save.
hint.oauthHelpManagedStep2: Copy the Client ID, Client Secret, and displayed OAuth URLs into the AI client's connector settings when it does not perform discovery automatically.
hint.oauthHelpManagedStep3: When connecting, the user sees Arthur's login page and signs in with an Arthur MCP account.
hint.oauthHelpFlowTitle: What happens when the AI connects
hint.oauthHelpFlow: The MCP client contacts the Connection URL, receives an authorization challenge, opens the configured login page, obtains a token, and sends it as Authorization: Bearer on every MCP request. The model only chooses tools; the MCP client handles authentication.
hint.oauthHelpTestStep1: Open the protected-resource metadata URL and confirm that it returns JSON with the expected resource, authorization server, and scopes.
hint.oauthHelpTestStep2: Connect through a compatible MCP client and complete login on the expected website. External mode must never display an Arthur password form.
hint.oauthHelpTestStep3: Call one tool after login. The request should succeed with a valid token and return 401 without credentials or with the wrong audience/scope.
hint.oauthClientSetup: Generate a Client ID and Client Secret, then configure the OAuth discovery, authorization, and token URLs shown in this panel.
hint.oauthClientLogin: With the authorization-code flow, users are prompted to sign in with their Arthur MCP account.
hint.oauthExternalPurpose: External OAuth redirects users to the customer's own login and consent pages; Arthur validates the tokens issued by that provider.
hint.oauthModelSafety: The AI model never receives passwords, authorization codes, client secrets, or access tokens; the MCP client handles them.
hint.oauthExternalLoginPage: Users will sign in on the external provider's website. Arthur will never collect their password.
hint.oauthManagedLoginPage: Arthur displays its own login page and issues the token. Use this mode for internal environments or testing.
hint.oauthJwks: Recommended for JWT access tokens. Configure JWKS or introspection.
hint.oauthIntrospection: Use for opaque tokens. Requires confidential introspection credentials.
hint.oauthAudience: Must identify this MCP resource. Tokens issued for another API are rejected.
hint.oauthScopes: Space- or comma-separated scopes that every MCP access token must contain.
hint.shareDesc: Send this link to anyone who needs to connect their AI client to this server. The page includes step-by-step instructions for Claude Desktop, Cursor, and a QR code for mobile.
hint.toolsDesc: Each tool is one specific action your AI can take.
hint.resourcesDesc: Content exposed to the AI via the MCP protocol (static or dynamic from API)
hint.promptsDesc: Prompts from your library that are active in this project.
hint.editPromptInLibrary: To edit the prompt content, go to the
hint.staticToolDesc: Static tools return a fixed response — optionally interpolating parameter values via {{paramName}}. No external connection is needed.
hint.noPromptInLibrary: No prompts in your library yet.
hint.allPromptsAdded: All prompts are already added to this server.
hint.createQueriesFirst: Create operations first in the Operations tab, then come back to create tools.
hint.noQueriesForTools: No operations defined yet. Create operations in the Operations tab first.
hint.inputSchemaBuiltFrom_one: Input schema will be built from {{count}} parameter: {{names}}
hint.inputSchemaBuiltFrom_other: Input schema will be built from {{count}} parameters: {{names}}
hint.chainInputHelp: Creates an input param on this chain
hint.selectToolAbove: Select a tool above to configure input mapping.
hint.addStepToStart: Click Add step to start building the chain. Each step calls a tool and passes its output to the next step.
hint.errorAlertsDesc: Receive an email when the error rate exceeds a threshold.
hint.constraintRequired: Rejects the call if this parameter is missing

empty.noEndpoints: No endpoints — upload an OpenAPI spec or click "Add endpoint" to create one manually.
empty.noResources: No resources yet. Click "New resource" to create a static resource or pick an endpoint to generate a dynamic one.
empty.noChains: No chains yet. Click New chain to create one.
empty.noToolsYet: No tools yet. Click "Add tool" to create one, or use "Update tools from spec" to import from an OpenAPI file.
empty.noStaticTools: No static tools yet. Click "New tool" to create one.
empty.noDbToolsWithQuery: No tools yet. Click "New tool" to expose an operation as an MCP tool.
empty.noPromptsAdded: No prompts added yet. Click Add prompt to include prompts from your library, or go to Prompt library to create new ones.
empty.noTablesFound: No tables found. Make sure your connection is correct and the database has tables.
empty.noCollectionsFound: No {{label}} found. Make sure your connection is correct.
empty.noToolsPermission: You don't have permission to view tools.
empty.noResourcesPermission: You don't have permission to view resources.
empty.noSettingsPermission: You don't have permission to manage server settings.
empty.noQueriesYet: No {{label}} yet. Click "Add {{singular}}" to define your first source operation, then create tools from it in the Tools tab.
empty.noTablesWarning: No tools available. Upload a spec first to create tool chains.
empty.noHooks: No hooks configured. Add a hook to inject headers, append query params, or log every call.
empty.noInputConstraints: No constraints defined. Add rules to validate or block specific parameter values.
empty.noOutputFilters: No output filters defined. Add rules to hide sensitive data from AI responses.
empty.noToolRestrictions: No tool restrictions defined. Add rules to block, limit, or gate specific tools.
empty.noFixedHeaders: No fixed headers yet. Add one to send it with every request.

confirm.revokeKeyMessage: Any client using this key will immediately lose access.
confirm.removeOAuthMessage: ChatGPT and any other OAuth clients will immediately lose access.
confirm.deleteEntityMessage: "{{name}}" will be permanently removed from this server.
confirm.deleteResourceMessage: "{{name}}" will be permanently removed from this server.
confirm.deleteChainMessage: This chain will be permanently removed and will no longer appear in the MCP server.
confirm.removePromptMessage: "{{name}}" will be removed from this server. The prompt will remain in your library.
confirm.deleteQueryMessage: Delete this {{label}}? Tools referencing it will stop working. This cannot be undone.

error.baseUrlSaveFailed: Failed to save. Please try again.
error.shareGenFailed: Could not generate the share link. Please try again.
error.introspectFailed: Introspection failed. Check your connection settings.

status.slugSaved: Slug saved. The share link now uses it — any previously shared link with the old slug stops working.

action.shareWithClient: Share the web documentation with client
action.uploadNewSpec: Upload a new version of the spec

tooltip.updateFromSpec: Upload a new version of the spec to add or update tools
tooltip.resumeServer: Resume — allow AI to use this server again
tooltip.pauseServer: Pause — block all AI requests to this server
tooltip.enableTool: Enable — make this tool available to the AI
tooltip.disableTool: Disable — hide this tool from the AI
tooltip.enableResource: Enable — make this resource available to the AI
tooltip.disableResource: Disable — hide this resource from the AI
tooltip.enablePrompt: Enable — make this prompt available to the AI
tooltip.disablePrompt: Disable — hide this prompt from the AI
tooltip.handlebarsDoc: Open Handlebars.js documentation — template syntax reference (expressions, #each, helpers)
tooltip.tenantRequired: Required — rejects calls missing this param

reimport.title: Re-import API spec
reimport.description: Upload a new version of the spec. Tools with the same name will be updated (schema + endpoint); new tools will be added. Existing tools not in the new spec are kept — delete them manually if needed.

logs.retention: Logs are kept for 7 days in memory and are not persisted across restarts.

baseUrl.title: API Base URL
baseUrl.helperText: Root URL used for every upstream API call.
baseUrl.errorInvalid: Enter a valid URL including http:// or https://.
baseUrl.errorSave: Arthur could not save the URL. Try again.

mcpEndpoint.intro: This is the address an MCP-compatible AI client uses to discover and call this server's enabled Tools, Resources, and Prompts.
mcpEndpoint.copy: Copy the endpoint exactly as shown.
mcpEndpoint.configure: Add a remote MCP server in your AI client and paste the endpoint as its server URL.
mcpEndpoint.authenticate: If Access Keys or OAuth are enabled, configure the matching credential in the AI client; otherwise the endpoint is public.
mcpEndpoint.result: After a successful connection, the AI client lists this server's enabled MCP capabilities and can call them during a conversation.
mcpEndpoint.troubleshoot: If discovery fails, check the public documentation slug, confirm the client can reach this Arthur host, and verify that its Access Key or OAuth configuration is current.

accessKeys.intro: Access Keys control which AI clients can connect to this MCP endpoint. Use them when the client supports a static credential and you do not need a user login flow.
accessKeys.create: Select Add key and give it a label that identifies the client or environment.
accessKeys.copy: Copy the new key immediately and store it in the AI client's secret or credential settings.
accessKeys.connect: Send it in the auth header when possible, or as ?auth=<key> when the client cannot set custom headers.
accessKeys.result: A valid key authorizes that client to use the MCP endpoint; an invalid or revoked key is rejected.
accessKeys.manage: Create a separate key for each client so you can revoke one connection without interrupting the others. With no keys, Access Keys do not protect the endpoint.
accessKeys.caution: Treat keys as passwords. Query-string keys may appear in browser history and infrastructure logs; prefer the header and revoke any exposed key.

baseUrl.intro: The root address of the upstream API Arthur calls for this server. Arthur combines it with each Tool path to build the final request URL.
baseUrl.environment: Moving the same Tools between environments, such as staging and production.
baseUrl.domain: The upstream API moves to another domain or subdomain.
baseUrl.version: Testing the same Tools against another API version hosted at a different root URL.
baseUrl.steps: Enter the protocol and host, plus any shared path prefix. Keep Tool-specific paths, path parameters, and query strings in each Tool. Save the field, then run a Tool to verify the complete URL.
baseUrl.result: The saved URL is applied to subsequent upstream calls from every Tool in this server.
baseUrl.troubleshoot: If calls return 404, check for a missing or duplicated path segment. For connection failures, verify DNS, protocol, port, and whether the Arthur host can reach the upstream API.

tools.intro: Each tool maps one API endpoint to a specific action your AI can take — for example 'search for a contact', 'create a ticket', or 'get order details'. The AI reads every tool name and description before deciding which one to call. A server with zero tools means the AI has nothing to do — create tools before connecting an AI client.
tools.naming: Tool names are the primary signal the AI uses to understand what each action does. Use short, specific names that describe both the action and its subject: get_customer_by_id, create_invoice, list_open_orders. Avoid generic names like do_action or api_call — the AI cannot reliably tell tools apart when names are vague or overlap with each other.
tools.betterDesc: Write descriptions the way you would explain the function to a junior developer. Instead of 'Get user', write 'Fetch a user account by their numeric ID — returns name, email, role, status, and last login date.' Mention what data comes back, what happens on failure, and any important limits such as 'max 100 results per page'. The AI uses descriptions to decide both whether to call a tool and how to fill in its parameters correctly.
tools.parameters: Each tool has input parameters that the AI fills in automatically from context. Give every parameter a clear name and description. 'user_id (integer): the numeric ID of the user to fetch' is far better than just 'id'. If a parameter only accepts a fixed set of values — like a status field — list all accepted values in the description so the AI never has to guess.
tools.disable: Use Disable to hide a tool from the AI without deleting it. The tool stays fully configured but becomes invisible to all AI clients. Useful when an endpoint is temporarily down, in maintenance, or too sensitive to expose to automation. Re-enable it instantly when you are ready — no reconfiguration needed.
tools.result: The expected result is a focused Tool catalog where an AI client can choose the correct operation and construct valid input without guessing.
tools.troubleshoot: If the AI calls the wrong tool: check that tool names are unique and unambiguous across the server. If the AI fills in parameters incorrectly: add concrete examples and the full list of accepted values to the parameter description. If a tool consistently returns errors: open the Activity Log tab to see the raw error response from the upstream API and diagnose from there.

activity.intro: A reverse-chronological record of every request made through this server. Each row is one tool call — showing which tool was used, where the request came from, whether it succeeded, how long the external API took to reply, and any error details.
activity.columns: Columns — Tool: which tool was called. Source: MCP (called by an AI client via the MCP protocol) or Direct (called via the REST API, typically from your own code or a test). Status: circle colour indicates success or failure. Time ms: how long the upstream API took to respond. Error: the message returned if the request failed.
activity.greenStatus: Green status — the upstream API responded with a 2xx HTTP code within the timeout. The tool call completed successfully and the result was returned to the AI.
activity.redStatus: Red status — the call failed. Most common causes: incorrect base URL, missing or expired authentication credentials, the upstream API returned a 4xx or 5xx error, or the request timed out. Click the row to expand the full error message.
activity.responseTime: Response time — how long the upstream API took to respond, in milliseconds. Under 1,000 ms is healthy. 1,000–3,000 ms turns orange and signals a slow upstream API. Over 3,000 ms turns red — consider raising the server default timeout in Settings, or investigate what is slowing the upstream service.
activity.sources: Source column — MCP means the call originated from an AI client (Claude, ChatGPT, a custom agent, etc.) through the MCP protocol. Direct means the call was made via the REST API, typically from your own code, a CI script, or a manual test with curl.
activity.whenErrors: Diagnosing red rows: note the HTTP status code in the error message. 401 or 403 means authentication is wrong or a key has expired. 404 means the endpoint path does not exist on the upstream API — check the tool path and the server base URL. 5xx means the upstream service itself is having problems. A timeout with no status code means Arthur gave up waiting — increase the timeout in Settings.
activity.result: Use this history after connecting a client or changing configuration: a successful test should create a green row for the expected Tool with a reasonable response time.
activity.retention: Logs are kept for 7 days and are not exportable from the UI. For longer retention or structured log access, configure your infrastructure to forward server logs to an external observability platform such as Datadog, Grafana Loki, or CloudWatch.

aiView.desc: This is the exact payload your AI client receives when it connects to this server over the MCP protocol — the full list of tools, their parameter schemas, and the descriptions the AI reads to understand what each action does. What you see here is precisely what the AI works from, nothing more and nothing less.
aiView.howAiDecides: When you send a message to the AI, it scans every tool name and description shown here to decide which tool to call and what values to pass as parameters. If two tools have similar names the AI may pick the wrong one. If a description is vague the AI may guess at parameter values — sometimes incorrectly. This view lets you audit exactly what the AI sees before a problem surfaces in production.
aiView.improving: To improve AI accuracy: make tool names unique and specific, add concrete examples in descriptions ('e.g. customer_id: 123 for John Smith'), list all accepted values for enum-style parameters, and describe what each tool returns and what errors it can raise. After editing tools, return to this view to verify the changes look exactly as intended — the AI sees the updated version immediately on its next connection.
aiView.result: When this view is clear and complete, connect the AI client and ask it to perform one narrow task. If it cannot find or correctly fill a Tool shown here, improve that Tool's name, description, or parameter schema.

responseLimit.intro: By default, Arthur passes the full upstream API response to the AI without any size restrictions. Enable this panel to activate truncation limits for this server only — useful when APIs return very large payloads that exceed your AI client's context window.
responseLimit.maxLenText: Hard character cap on the entire response body after serialisation. Responses longer than this are cut off with a truncation notice.
responseLimit.maxDepthText: Objects nested deeper than this level are collapsed to "[...]" to reduce token usage for deeply nested APIs.
responseLimit.arraySliceText: Arrays are sliced to the first N items. A note is appended showing how many items were omitted.
responseLimit.errorLenText: Maximum length of the body included in error responses (HTTP 4xx/5xx). Keep it short to avoid flooding the AI with long error pages.
responseLimit.toggleText: Changes save automatically. Disable the toggle to turn off all limits and restore full raw responses.
responseLimit.steps: Enable limits, start with values that preserve the fields the AI needs, and test a representative large response. Adjust one limit at a time if important data is omitted.
responseLimit.result: The AI receives a smaller response with truncation indicators instead of an oversized payload.
responseLimit.troubleshoot: If the AI loses required context, increase the relevant limit or narrow the upstream response. Disabling this panel restores the full upstream payload.

rateLimit.intro: Caps the number of MCP requests this server accepts per minute. When the limit is exceeded, the server responds with HTTP 429 (Too Many Requests) and a Retry-After header — the AI client should wait before retrying.
rateLimit.protectText: many APIs have their own rate limits. Staying within them prevents your API credentials from being throttled or suspended.
rateLimit.costText: every call to a paid API costs money. A rate limit prevents AI agents from accidentally making thousands of calls in a loop.
rateLimit.runawayText: AI agents in agentic workflows can sometimes get stuck in retry loops. A rate limit acts as a circuit breaker.
rateLimit.fairText: if multiple AI clients share this endpoint, a limit ensures no single client monopolises the quota.
rateLimit.howTitle: How to set the right limit:
rateLimit.howText: check your upstream API's documented rate limit (e.g. 100 req/min) and set Arthur's limit slightly below it to leave headroom. Start conservative and increase if the AI frequently hits 429.
rateLimit.toggleText: Toggle the switch to Inactive to disable rate limiting entirely. Changes save automatically.
rateLimit.result: Requests within the configured minute proceed normally; excess requests receive HTTP 429 and Retry-After.
rateLimit.troubleshoot: If legitimate traffic receives frequent 429 responses, compare Activity with the upstream quota and raise the limit only when the upstream service and budget can support it.

retryPolicy.intro: When a tool call fails, Arthur can automatically retry it before returning an error to the AI.
retryPolicy.fixed: waits the same delay between every retry.
retryPolicy.linear: increases the delay by the initial delay on each attempt (e.g. 500ms -> 1000ms -> 1500ms).
retryPolicy.exponential: doubles the delay on each attempt (e.g. 500ms -> 1000ms -> 2000ms). Best for rate-limit errors.
retryPolicy.safeCodes: Only retry on codes that are safe to retry (idempotent calls). Avoid retrying POST calls that modify state unless the upstream API is idempotent.
retryPolicy.steps: Enable the policy, choose the maximum retries and initial delay, select a backoff strategy, then list only the HTTP status codes your upstream API says are transient.
retryPolicy.result: Arthur retries matching failures automatically and returns either the first successful response or the final error after the retry budget is exhausted.
retryPolicy.caution: Retries increase latency and can repeat side effects. Use Activity to confirm which status is recurring; do not retry state-changing calls unless the upstream API guarantees idempotency.

timeout.intro: Sets the maximum time Arthur waits for a tool call to complete before returning a timeout error to the AI.
timeout.overrides: The global timeout applies to all tools. Per-tool overrides let you give more time to known slow endpoints (e.g. a report generation endpoint) or less time to fast ones.
timeout.upstream: If the upstream API has its own timeout, set Arthur's lower so the AI receives a clean error rather than a dropped connection.
timeout.steps: Set a global timeout for normal calls, then add overrides only for Tools with a proven need for more or less time. Values are in milliseconds.
timeout.result: Calls that exceed their effective timeout stop and return a clear timeout error to the AI client.
timeout.troubleshoot: If healthy slow calls time out, inspect Activity and raise only the affected Tool's override. If calls hang too long, lower the value and investigate the upstream service.

executionHooks.intro: Hooks run automatically before or after every tool call on this server.
executionHooks.inject: adds a fixed header to every upstream API request (e.g. X-Tenant-ID).
executionHooks.query: appends a fixed query parameter to every request URL.
executionHooks.log: records phase, tool name, and timestamp to the activity log — useful for auditing without custom code.
executionHooks.phases: Before hooks run before the request is sent. After hooks run after the response is received. Disabled hooks are saved but not executed.
executionHooks.steps: Add a hook, choose its phase and action, fill the name and value required by that action, then leave it enabled and run a Tool to verify the effect.
executionHooks.result: Enabled hooks apply consistently to each matching phase without changing individual Tool definitions.
executionHooks.caution: Hooks affect every Tool in this server. Avoid duplicate headers or query parameters, never place secrets in logged values, and disable a hook while diagnosing unexpected upstream requests.

inputConstraints.intro: Rules that validate or block tool call parameters before the request reaches the upstream API.
inputConstraints.required: rejects the call if the parameter is absent.
inputConstraints.blockValue: rejects calls where the parameter equals the blocked value.
inputConstraints.maxLength: rejects string values longer than the limit.
inputConstraints.allowedValues: rejects values not in a comma-separated whitelist.
inputConstraints.regex: rejects values that do not match the regular expression.
inputConstraints.allTools: Use * as tool name to apply a rule to all tools.
inputConstraints.steps: Choose a Tool or *, enter the exact parameter name, select the constraint, supply its comparison value when required, and enable the rule.
inputConstraints.result: Invalid input is rejected before Arthur sends any request to the upstream API.
inputConstraints.caution: Parameter names and regex patterns must match exactly. Test both an accepted and rejected value; an overly broad rule can block valid calls across all Tools.

outputFiltering.intro: Rules that scrub, mask, or remove sensitive data from tool responses before the AI sees them.
outputFiltering.mask: replaces a field's value with a fixed string (e.g. *****). Use to hide secrets while keeping the structure intact.
outputFiltering.remove: deletes the field entirely from the response. Use for internal metadata the AI does not need.
outputFiltering.redact: replaces any string matching a regex with a placeholder. Use to scrub credit card numbers, SSNs, or tokens anywhere in the response.
outputFiltering.paths: Field paths use dot-notation (e.g. data.user.email). Use * as tool name to apply a rule to all tools.
outputFiltering.steps: Choose a Tool or *, select how to filter, enter the exact field path or regex, and enable the rule. Test with a response that contains the sensitive value.
outputFiltering.result: Arthur transforms the upstream response before returning it, so the AI receives the masked, removed, or redacted version.
outputFiltering.caution: Filtering is not a substitute for limiting upstream access. If data still appears, inspect the real response shape and correct the field path or regex; broad regexes can hide useful content.

toolRestrictions.intro: Rules that restrict how specific tools can be invoked by the AI.
toolRestrictions.blocked: prevents the AI from calling this tool entirely. The tool is still visible in the schema but returns an error if invoked.
toolRestrictions.maxCalls: limits how many times the AI can invoke a tool within a single conversation session. Useful for expensive or destructive operations.
toolRestrictions.confirmation: pauses execution and asks the human user to confirm before the tool is called. Recommended for any tool that writes, deletes, or sends data.
toolRestrictions.disabled: Disabled restrictions are saved but not enforced.
toolRestrictions.steps: Choose a Tool, select the restriction, provide its limit when required, and enable it. Use * only when every Tool should receive the same restriction.
toolRestrictions.result: Arthur checks the restriction before execution and rejects or pauses calls that do not satisfy it.
toolRestrictions.caution: Test from the intended AI client. If a restriction seems ignored, confirm it is enabled and targets the exact Tool name; if legitimate calls stop, check whether a * rule also applies.

harness.title: Harness
harness.subtitle: Configure execution hooks, retry policies, and timeout settings for this server's tools.

section.hooksDesc: Run custom logic before and after each tool call — inject headers, transform inputs, log results, or trigger side-effects without touching the upstream API.
section.retryPolicyDesc: Define how many times a failed tool call should be retried, with which backoff strategy, and for which HTTP error codes.
section.timeoutsDesc: Set per-tool or server-wide timeout limits to prevent slow upstream APIs from stalling the AI's responses.

harness.empty: No harness configuration yet. Execution hooks, retry policies, and timeouts will be managed here.

guardRails.title: Guard Rails
guardRails.subtitle: Define safety rules that constrain what the AI is allowed to send and receive through this server's tools.

section.inputConstraintsDesc: Validate or reject tool call parameters before they reach the upstream API — block forbidden values, enforce allowed ranges, or require specific formats.
section.outputFilteringDesc: Scrub sensitive data from tool responses before they are returned to the AI — mask PII, redact secrets, or strip fields the model should not see.
section.toolRestrictionsDesc: Restrict which tools can be called under certain conditions — by user role, time of day, rate limits, or any custom rule.

guardRails.empty: No guard rails configured yet. Input constraints, output filters, and tool restrictions will be managed here.

auth.title: API Credentials
auth.helpIntro: The credentials Arthur automatically attaches to every outgoing HTTP request when calling the upstream API on behalf of an AI tool call. The AI never sees or handles these credentials — Arthur injects them invisibly.
auth.helpChoose: Choose the mode that matches your API's requirements:
auth.helpNone: None: Public API — no credentials are attached. Use for unauthenticated endpoints.
auth.helpBearer: Bearer Token: Adds the header Authorization: Bearer <token>. Most modern REST APIs and OAuth2 resource servers use this.
auth.helpApiKey: API Key: Adds the key as a custom header (e.g. X-API-Key) or as a query parameter. Check your API's documentation for the exact field name.
auth.helpBasic: Basic Auth: Adds Authorization: Basic <base64(username:password)>. Used by some legacy APIs and services like Jira or Confluence.
auth.helpOAuth: OAuth2 Client Credentials: Arthur fetches a Bearer token automatically using your client ID and secret, and renews it before it expires. Use for machine-to-machine integrations where no user is involved.
auth.helpCustom: Custom Headers: Add any arbitrary HTTP headers. Useful for APIs with non-standard authentication schemes or when you need to pass multiple headers.
auth.helpSteps: Choose the authentication type documented by the upstream API, complete the fields it exposes, wait for the saved indicator, then run a Tool that requires authentication.
auth.helpResult: Arthur adds the configured credential only when it calls the upstream API; the AI client receives the Tool result without receiving this credential.
auth.helpSecurity: Important security note: credentials are stored encrypted in the database. Use tokens and keys with the minimum required scope — if a credential is exposed, a limited-scope key reduces the blast radius.
auth.helpSeparation: These credentials are completely separate from the MCP Authentication key (which protects the MCP endpoint). One controls who can call Arthur; the other controls how Arthur calls your API.
auth.helpTroubleshoot: For upstream 401 or 403 errors, verify the selected type, header or parameter name, token expiry, scopes, and client credentials. MCP connection errors belong to Access Keys or OAuth Client instead.
auth.noneHint: The API is public and does not require authentication. No header will be added.
auth.oauthHint: Uses client_credentials. Token is fetched automatically and renewed when it expires.
auth.customHint: Add any HTTP header to the request (e.g. X-Tenant-Id, X-Auth-Token).
auth.storedWarning: Credentials are stored in the database. Use tokens with minimum required scope.
